stem: The matrix \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[c]] & [[b]] \\ [[e]] & [[c]] \end{pmatrix} \) has \( \lambda=[[c]] \) as a repeated eigenvalue (algebraic multiplicity 2). To decide whether \( A \) is diagonalizable, compute \( \operatorname{rank}(A - [[c]]I) \) (its geometric multiplicity is \( 2 - \operatorname{rank}(A-[[c]]I) \)). What is \( \operatorname{rank}(A-[[c]]I) \)?
solution: \( A - [[c]]I = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & [[b]] \\ [[e]] & 0 \end{pmatrix} \); this is the zero matrix only if both off-diagonal entries vanish (rank 0), and otherwise has exactly one independent row (rank 1). Here \( \operatorname{rank}(A-[[c]]I) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: The rank of a \(2\times2\) matrix with a zero diagonal and \(bc=0\) can be at most 1, never 2.
The rank of any \(2\times2\) matrix can never exceed 2.
A rank can never be negative.
The rank of any \(2\times2\) matrix can never exceed 2.
A rank can never be negative.

stem: The triangular matrix \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[c]] & [[p]] & [[q]] \\ 0 & [[c]] & [[r]] \\ 0 & 0 & [[d]] \end{pmatrix} \) has \( \lambda=[[c]] \) as a repeated eigenvalue (algebraic multiplicity 2). What is \( \operatorname{rank}(A-[[c]]I) \) (this determines the geometric multiplicity of \( \lambda=[[c]] \), and hence whether \( A \) is diagonalizable)?
solution: \( A - [[c]]I = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & [[p]] & [[q]] \\ 0 & 0 & [[r]] \\ 0 & 0 & [[dc]] \end{pmatrix} \), which has rank [[answer]] (the first column is entirely zero; since \( [[dc]] \ne 0\) the third column always contributes one pivot, and the second column contributes an extra pivot exactly when \( [[p]] \ne 0 \)).
distractor_rationales: Since \( [[c]] \ne [[d]] \), the last row is not entirely zero, so the rank cannot be 0.
The first column of \( A-[[c]]I \) is entirely zero, so the rank can never reach the full size 3.
The rank of a \(3\times3\) matrix can never exceed 3.
A rank can never be negative.
A rank can never be negative.
The rank of a \(3\times3\) matrix can never exceed 3.

stem: The triangular matrix \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[c]] & [[p]] & [[q]] \\ 0 & [[c]] & [[r]] \\ 0 & 0 & [[c]] \end{pmatrix} \) has \( \lambda=[[c]] \) as its only eigenvalue, with algebraic multiplicity 3. What is \( \operatorname{rank}(A-[[c]]I) \) (this determines the geometric multiplicity of \( \lambda=[[c]] \), and hence whether \( A \) is diagonalizable)?
solution: \( A - [[c]]I = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & [[p]] & [[q]] \\ 0 & 0 & [[r]] \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \) is strictly upper triangular, so it can never be invertible (rank \( <3 \)); its exact rank is [[answer]], determined by how many of \( [[p]], [[r]] \) are nonzero.
distractor_rationales: A strictly upper-triangular \(3\times3\) matrix always has a zero row, which forces rank below 3.
The rank of a \(3\times3\) matrix can never exceed 3.
A rank can never be negative.
A rank can never be negative.
The rank of a \(3\times3\) matrix can never exceed 3.

stem: The matrix \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[c]] & 0 \\ 0 & [[d]] \end{pmatrix} \) is already diagonal, so it is diagonalizable with \( D=A \). As a check, what is \( \operatorname{rank}(A-[[c]]I) \) (the amount by which the rank falls short of full rank gives the geometric multiplicity of \( \lambda=[[c]] \))?
solution: \( A - [[c]]I = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & [[d]]-[[c]] \end{pmatrix} \); since \( [[c]] \ne [[d]] \), this matrix has exactly one nonzero row, so its rank is [[answer]] (matching the geometric multiplicity \( 2-1=1 \) of the simple eigenvalue \( \lambda=[[c]] \)).
distractor_rationales: The rank of any \(2\times2\) matrix can never exceed 2.
The rank of any \(2\times2\) matrix can never exceed 2.
A rank can never be negative.
The rank of any \(2\times2\) matrix can never exceed 2.
